title: Deep Mantle Deformation Linked to Ancient Slabs
summary: Scientists have created a global map showing deformation in Earth's lowermost mantle, located just above the core-mantle boundary. Using over 16 million seismograms, they analyzed seismic anisotropy—the directional variation of shear wave speeds—to identify areas where mantle material has been stretched or distorted. The study, published in The Seismic Record, reveals that most deformation occurs in regions where ancient tectonic slabs have subducted and sunk over millions of years. This finding supports geodynamic simulations and provides new insight into mantle flow patterns. Lead author Jonathan Wolf noted that while upper mantle deformation is driven by plate drag, deep mantle flow is less understood. This research offers a large-scale view of deep Earth dynamics.

keywords.explanation: The property of materials that causes seismic waves to travel at different speeds depending on direction.
The deepest part of the mantle, just above the core-mantle boundary.
Pieces of tectonic plate that have descended into the mantle.

questions.question: What is the lowermost mantle?
options: The top layer of the mantle
The deepest part of the mantle near the core
questions.correct_answer: The deepest part of the mantle near the core

questions.question: What is seismic anisotropy?
options: The variation of wave speed with direction
questions.correct_answer: The variation of wave speed with direction

Where does most deep mantle deformation occur?

background_read: The mantle is the thick layer between Earth's crust and core. Convection currents in the mantle drive plate tectonics. Seismic waves from earthquakes help scientists study Earth's interior. Anisotropy indicates deformation or flow alignment.
Article_Structure: This research provides a global map of deep mantle deformation. The main points include that deformation is concentrated where ancient slabs have sunk, supporting geodynamic models. The purpose is to understand mantle flow dynamics. Evidence evaluation: the dataset of 16 million seismograms is robust and comprehensive. Author credibility: Jonathan Wolf is a researcher at UC Berkeley. Methodology: analysis of seismic anisotropy using multiple wave phases.

perspectives.description: This study advances understanding of mantle dynamics and supports existing simulations.
The use of a massive dataset demonstrates the power of big data in geoscience.
